title: Introduction
text: The way you project your graph determines what algorithms can "see" and analyze. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s046-000-existing] Let's take a look at this flowchart. It illustrates how different projections can alter the visibility and analysis capabilities of algorithms. In this session, you'll learn how to create projections, understand graph structures, and why this matters for your algorithm results.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to: Create Cypher projections using `gds.graph.project()`, distinguish between graph structure and node labels in GDS, identify monopartite, bipartite, multipartite, and heterogeneous graph structures, and choose appropriate projection strategies based on your target algorithm.

title: Running a Cypher Projection
text: The most basic Cypher projection command looks like this: MATCH (source:Actor)-[r:ACTED_IN]->(target:Movie) // Match these nodes and relationships WITH gds.graph.project( 'actors-graph', source, target ) AS g RETURN g.graphName AS graph, g.nodeCount AS nodes, g.relationshipCount AS rels. This command helps you create a projection by identifying source and target nodes and returning the properties of the graph.

title: Projecting Graph Models
text: You are not limited to using the relationships available in the main graph. For example, you can use intermediate nodes in your MATCH statement to create new relationships that exist only in the projection. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s049-001-existing] Notice how this diagram shows the use of intermediate nodes in graph projections, allowing for the creation of new relationships. MATCH (source:Actor)-[r:ACTED_IN]-> (:Movie) <-[:ACTED_IN]-(target:Actor) WITH gds.graph.project( 'actors-graph', source, target ) AS g RETURN g.graphName AS graph, g.nodeCount AS nodes, g.relationshipCount AS rels.

title: Actor to Actor Collaboration Graph
text: Running the previous projection will create a graph connecting actors directly to actors who worked on the same movies. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s050-002-existing] Here, you can see a projected graph of actors who worked with other actors. Run the following basic graph projection to see how this works for real: MATCH (source:Actor)-[r:ACTED_IN]->(target:Movie) WITH gds.graph.project( 'actors-graph', source, target ) AS g RETURN g.graphName AS graph, g.nodeCount AS nodes, g.relationshipCount AS rels.

title: What You Projected
text: Let's focus on the first projection you ran: MATCH (source:Actor)-[r:ACTED_IN]->(target:Movie) WITH gds.graph.project( 'actors-graph', source, target ) AS g RETURN g.graphName AS graph, g.nodeCount AS nodes, g.relationshipCount AS rels. This command helps you understand the basic structure of your projected graph.

title: What You Expected
text: You may have expected to project a graph that looks like this: [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s052-002-existing] This is a bipartite graph—a graph whose nodes fall into two distinct, non-overlapping sets. In this case, Actors and Movies.

title: What GDS Actually Sees
text: By default, GDS strips away labels but preserves structure: [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s053-003-existing] The graph is still structurally bipartite—Actors still only connect to Movies, never to other Actors. But GDS no longer knows which nodes are Actors and which are Movies.

title: Structure vs Labels
text: Two separate concepts: Structure: How nodes connect (bipartite, monopartite, etc.) Labels: What GDS knows about node types. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s054-003-new] Your projection kept the bipartite structure but lost the labels.

title: Graph Structures: Monopartite
text: A monopartite graph has nodes that cannot be separated into distinct non-overlapping sets. Example: A social network where (:Person)-[:FRIENDS_WITH]->(:Person). [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s055-004-new] Any person can be friends with any other person. You cannot divide the nodes into separate groups where connections only occur between groups.

title: Graph Structures: Bipartite
text: A bipartite graph has nodes that fall into exactly two non-overlapping sets, where connections only occur between sets. Example: (:Actor)-[:ACTED_IN]->(:Movie). [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s056-003-existing] Actors connect to Movies. Actors never connect directly to other Actors. Movies never connect directly to other Movies.

title: Graph Structures: Multipartite
text: A multipartite graph has three or more non-overlapping node sets, where connections only occur between sets, never within the same set. Example: (:User)-[:RATES]->(:Movie)-[:IN_GENRE]->(:Genre). [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s057-004-existing] In a true multipartite structure, each set is non-overlapping.

title: Graph Structures: Heterogeneous
text: A heterogeneous graph has multiple node types and/or relationship types, but nodes within the same type can connect to each other. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s058-005-existing] Our full Movies dataset has Actors, Movies, Users, and Genres. Movies can connect to all node types, meaning the connections can overlap.

title: Why Structure Matters: PageRank Example
text: PageRank ranks nodes by "importance" based on incoming connections from other important nodes. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s059-006-existing] Let's see what happens when we run it on our unlabelled bipartite projection: CALL gds.pageRank.stream('actors-graph', {}) YIELD nodeId, score RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).title, score ORDER BY score DESC.

title: PageRank on Bipartite Structure
text: In our Actor → Movie bipartite structure, PageRank flows into Movies but has nowhere to go from there. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s060-007-existing] As shown in this diagram, a single movie node with many actor nodes pointing into it illustrates the PageRank flow in a bipartite structure.

title: Modelling, Not Algorithms
text: It's important to remember here: we are talking about graph structures, using algorithms for framing. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s061-008-existing] Do not worry too much about the intricacies of PageRank or any other algorithm for now—that comes later. For now, try to see how the signal flows from node to node in the graph structures we're examining.

title: Rank Sink
text: In our bipartite graph, Movie nodes become "rank sinks"—accumulating high scores simply because they receive connections, not because they're meaningfully important. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s062-005-new] Almost all nodes receive the same score on either side of the structure. The bipartite structure traps the algorithm's ranking signal.

title: Solution 1: Project a True Monopartite Graph
text: Now let's return to that second projection—the Actor-to-Actor collaboration graph: [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s063-009-existing] This diagram shows Actor-to-Actor collaboration with invisible Movie bridges. MATCH (source:Actor)-[:ACTED_IN]->(:Movie)<-[:ACTED_IN]-(target:Actor) WITH gds.graph.project('actors-only', source, target) AS g RETURN g.graphName, g.nodeCount, g.relationshipCount. This creates direct Actor-to-Actor connections through shared Movies. The Movies become invisible "bridges."

title: True Monopartite Result
text: The projected graph is now monopartite. All actors connect to other actors. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s064-010-existing] There is no meaningful way of separating the nodes into non-overlapping sets. As you can see, actors are connected directly to each other.

title: PageRank on Monopartite
text: Now PageRank can flow between nodes of the same type, producing meaningful importance rankings. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s065-011-existing] This illustration shows a slightly transparent Movie node, no longer part of the graph, with multiple relationships crossing over it.

title: Monopartite Structures
text: Bear in mind, the projection still does not retain node labels. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s066-006-new] It is the graph structure, not its labels, that affects the algorithm's results.

title: Preserve Labels
text: For some algorithms, you will want to retain node labels. Use configuration to preserve labels: MATCH (source:Actor)-[r:ACTED_IN]->(target:Movie) WITH gds.graph.project( 'actors-movies-labelled', source, target, { sourceNodeLabels: labels(source), targetNodeLabels: labels(target), relationshipType: type(r) }, {} ) AS g RETURN g.graphName, g.nodeCount, g.relationshipCount.

title: When to Preserve Labels
text: Preserve labels when: You need to filter algorithms by node type, or node type distinctions affect your analysis. Use default (unlabelled) when: You're projecting a true monopartite or bipartite subgraph, or the algorithm ignores node labels (most do). [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s068-008-new] This flowchart guides you on when to preserve labels versus using default unlabelled projections, based on algorithm requirements.

title: Bipartite-Friendly Algorithm: Node Similarity
text: Node Similarity compares nodes based on shared neighbours. It's designed for bipartite graphs. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s069-012-existing] This diagram shows how Node Similarity works on a bipartite graph with shared neighbours. MATCH (source:User)-[r:RATED]->(target:Movie) WITH gds.graph.project( 'user-rated-movie', source, target, { sourceNodeLabels: labels(source), targetNodeLabels: labels(target) }, {} ) AS g RETURN g.graphName, g.nodeCount, g.relationshipCount.

title: Node Similarity Result
text: Node Similarity finds Users who rated similar Movies—creating new User-to-User relationships. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-4-graph-types-s070-009-new] The algorithm respects the bipartite structure: it compares nodes on one side based on their connections to the other side.

title: Quick Reference: Choosing Your Projection
text: Algorithm Type |Graph Structure |Projection Strategy: PageRank, Betweenness works best on monopartite, project single node type (e.g., Actor-to-Actor). Node Similarity is designed for bipartite, preserve labels, include both types. Community Detection varies by algorithm, check documentation for each.

title: Common Terminology
text: Term |Meaning: Monopartite means nodes cannot be separated into distinct non-overlapping sets. Bipartite means exactly two non-overlapping node sets; connections only between sets. Multipartite means three or more non-overlapping node sets. Heterogeneous means multiple node types and/or relationship types (may overlap). Unlabelled means GDS doesn't know node/relationship types (default behaviour).

title: Lesson Summary
text: In this lesson, you learned: How to create Cypher projections with `gds.graph.project()`, how to transform graph structures by changing your MATCH pattern, structure (how nodes connect) is separate from labels (what GDS knows about types), GDS strips labels by default but preserves structure, bipartite structures can trap algorithms like PageRank, project true monopartite graphs for algorithms that expect them, and preserve labels when using bipartite-aware algorithms like Node Similarity. In the next lesson, you'll practice projecting different graph types.
